_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: A yield curve represents Treasury yields plotted across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with a normal upward slope indicating higher yields for longer maturities. An inverted yield curve slopes downward, historically preceding US recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.56% for June 2026, and the 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread is 0.38 points, indicating a positive slope. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the 2s10s spread was inverted, reaching 0.0 points at the deepest inversion, signifying a historical recession signal that has since reversed.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a key recession indicator, with inversion below 0.0 points historically preceding every modern US recession. The inversion persisted through 2022 to 2024 but has since re-steepened to 0.38 points, indicating the curve has un-inverted. The New York Fed's recession probability currently stands at 31%, a moderate level, reflecting some risk but not certainty. Notably, the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession begins, which complicates timing based solely on this metric.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve's positive 2s10s spread and the 4.56% 10-year yield suggest reduced immediate recession signal strength compared to recent years of inversion. Combined with a 31% recession probability from the NY Fed, the data suggests some ongoing economic risk but no definitive timing for downturn onset.
